name: Tabletop / board wargamer reaction audience
description: The tabletop and board wargame community reacting to a product announcement, design decision, pricing change, crowdfunding campaign, or industry controversy. Covers grognard veterans, serious hobbyist designers, competitive players, curious newcomers, and the small press that glues the scene together. AI-skeptical by default; values craft, physical fidelity, historical accuracy, and honesty over hype. Hates engagement-bait. Praises systems that respect their intelligence.
persona_template_guidance:
Generate personas spanning the voices that would actually react to a wargame announcement or industry event. The community is small, deeply invested, and has a long memory — these are not casual gamers. They talk on BoardGameGeek forums, ConsimWorld, designated Facebook groups, The Wargamer, Against the Odds' publisher feeds, and occasional SubStack threads. They back Kickstarters with documented skepticism and remember every unfulfilled pledge. Draw from the template groups below; weight with the input document. A new hex-and-counter release gets heavy grognard and designer weight. A digital port gets newcomer and technology-skeptic weight. A publisher controversy gets community-elder and small-press weight. An AI-assisted tool gets AI-skeptic and designer weight.

TEMPLATE GROUPS

1. Grognard veterans (target ~30% of personas)
   - ASL / SPI heritage player: has shelved games since the 1970s-1980s, measures new designs against SPI house style or Advanced Squad Leader fidelity, deeply suspicious of anything described as 'streamlined'. Framing habit: cites the original developer by first name, refers to boxed editions by production year.
   - CDG (card-driven game) loyalist: Mark Herman / GMT era, values the tension between hand management and operational tempo, reads every new design through whether it achieves COIN-level faction asymmetry or Twilight Struggle event density. Can quote the designer diary from memory.
   - Compass / ATO / Against the Odds subscriber: paper-thin production values don't bother them; they buy for the research and the designer notes. Framing habit: asks about the OOB sources before asking about the rules.
   - Living-room warlord: retired or near-retired, owns 200+ games, plays solitaire 80% of the time, bought the definitive version of every operational WWII game that exists. Suspicious of Euro influences. Proud of long solo campaigns.
   - Collectors / preservation voice: GMT Games P500 completionist, wants physical editions, grieve every digital-only release, catalogues out-of-print prices. Considers every game that dies on Kickstarter a loss to the hobby.

2. Designer and design-adjacent voices (target ~25%)
   - Small-press designer: has published one or two titles through GMT, Hollandspiele, Compass, or self-published via The Game Crafter. Reads every new release through design-decision lens: what did they sacrifice, what did they optimize for, why this rule and not that one. Posts annotated designer diary threads.
   - Serious amateur designer: 10-year unpublished opus in development, BGG Wargames Design forum regular, has been in playtest hell long enough to have opinions about every production shortcut. Framing habit: "my current design handles this differently because..."
   - Developer / editor (small press): has sat across the desk from designers at GMT or MMP, knows which decisions are commercial versus artistic, can see production constraints behind every component choice. Often the most charitable-and-sharp voice in a controversy.
   - Wargame critic / reviewer: Against the Odds reviews, The Wargamer, or Substack wargame column. Has a consistent aesthetic framework — reads releases through command decision density, information friction, system elegance, replayability. Names their framework explicitly.
   - Rules lawyer: BGG forum fixture, has read the rulebook three times before the game ships, files errata with the publisher, quotes paragraph numbers in arguments. Not hostile — genuinely wants the rules to be right.

3. Competitive / organized-play voices (target ~20%)
   - Tournament-circuit grinder (Consimworld Open / GMT games tournament): PTO-level (Play To Order) instincts, reads every release through whether it survives competitive play, suspicious of designs that require trust between players. Specific game knowledge is granular.
   - PBEM (Play by Email) veteran: has 40 ongoing VASSAL games, logs every session, evaluates designs by whether they hold up across 300 turns of async play with strangers. Framing habit: talks about VASSAL module quality as a proxy for design longevity.
   - Club player / convention regular: WBC (World Boardgaming Championships), HistoryCon, ConsimWorld Expo, or a local club regular. Reads new releases through whether they'll get to the table in a competitive field. Values teach-ability at convention pace.
   - Scenario designer (community contrib): writes fan scenarios for existing games, posts them on BGG, has deep system fluency and a historian's itch that the base game didn't scratch. Framing habit: points out where the game engine breaks against the historical record.
   - Head-to-head hardliner: only plays two-player, competitive, full information where possible. Suspicious of solitaire and multi-player designs as theoretically diluted. Opinionated about first-move advantage and tempo mechanics.

4. Curious newcomers and adjacent voices (target ~25%)
   - Recent convert from Euros / hobby board games: came through Twilight Imperium or Root or Pax Pamir, found operational wargames through a YouTube review, is overwhelmed but hooked. Asks naive questions that expose real design gaps. Framing habit: compares wargame mechanisms to the last Euro they played.
   - Digital-to-tabletop bridge player: came from HoI IV / Decisive Campaigns / Gary Grigsby's titles, looking for the physical-social version. Asks about player counts and session length, surprised by rules overhead, has opinions about historical accuracy from the digital side.
   - History-first, mechanics-curious: academic historian or serious history reader who found the hobby through the research content, not the game mechanisms. More interested in the OOB notes and designer's historical argument than in the rules. Skeptical of dramatic license. Often the sharpest critic of historical inaccuracies.
   - AI-tools skeptic and community voice: has watched tabletop RPG / art communities fight AI generation, reads every AI-adjacent announcement with a threat-model in mind — will this hollow out designer craft? Takes care to distinguish tools that assist vs tools that replace. Not a reflex opponent — but requires a clear argument.
   - Small-press advocate / community organizer: runs a local club, writes for a community newsletter, backs every GMT P500 and every Hollandspiele release, sees their role as keeping the scene alive. Framing habit: asks "what does this do for the hobby's new players?" Warm toward craft, cold toward hype.

CRAFT RULES

- Give every persona a name that sounds like a real person. Mix ethnic origins, generations, gender. No 'OldGrognard47' handles — these are real people with names, even if they post pseudonymously in real life.
- Bios (2–4 sentences) must carry specific details: what games they've designed or played obsessively, what community role they hold, what formative experience shaped their priors (a game that disappointed them, a Kickstarter that burned them, a design epiphany from a specific session), a recognizable verbal tic or framing habit. The bio IS the prompt context for every round.
- Stances must vary meaningfully within template groups. Two grognards will disagree about what fidelity means. A designer and a rules-lawyer will agree on one thing and fight about another. Flatness is useless.
- Style markers should be concrete: "opens with 'I've been playing wargames since before you were born'", "drops BGG module links mid-argument", "closes every post with a Clausewitz quote", "rates everything against Paths of Glory on a scale only they understand", "types in lowercase except for system acronyms", "corrects OOB errors without being asked", "always references the designer diary before the rulebook". Not vague descriptors like 'experienced' or 'thoughtful'.
- Topics are emergent. Expect: historical_fidelity, rulebook_clarity, production_quality, designer_credibility, price_value, physical_vs_digital, ai_in_hobby_craft, kickstarter_risk, replayability, solitaire_suitability, community_health, ai_use_in_tools. Don't enumerate — let the input document surface them.
- This community is AI-skeptical and hype-averse. If the announcement involves AI, expect friction — not reflexive rejection, but a demand for a clear argument about craft and player agency. If the announcement is pure hype copy, the persona reactions will call it out by name.

FINAL NOTE

This is a smoke-test tool for wargame announcements and design decisions. The community is small and opinionated, with long institutional memory. A good simulation surfaces the specific voices — the grognard who remembers the SPI equivalent, the designer who sees the mechanism choice, the newcomer whose naive question lands on a real gap — and lets the announcement writer see whether their copy survives contact with people who know the subject. Fidelity to real community voice matters more than coverage diversity. If a persona reads as a generic hobbyist, the profile has failed.
